coredata.dc:title: High quantum yield graphene quantum dots decorated TiO2 nanotubes for enhancing photocatalytic activity
coredata.dc:creator: Qu, Ailan
Xie, Haolong
Xu, Xinmei
Zhang, Yangyu
Wen, Shengwu
Cui, Yifan
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with high quantum yield (about 23.6% at an excitation wavelength of 320nm) and GQDs/TiO2 nanotubes (GQDs/TiO2 NTs) composites were achieved by a simple hydrothermal method at low temperature. Photoluminescence characterization showed that the GQDs exhibited the down-conversion PL features at excitation from 300 to 420nm and up-conversion photoluminescence in the range of 600–800nm. The photocatalytic activity of prepared GQDs/TiO2 NTs composites on the degradation of methyl orange (MO) was significantly enhanced compared with that of pure TiO2 nanotubes (TiO2 NTs). For the composites coupling with 1.5%, 2.5% and 3.5% GQDs, the degradation of MO after 20min irradiation under UV–vis light irradiation (λ=380–780nm) were 80.52%, 94.64% and 51.91%, respectively, which are much higher than that of pure TiO2 NTs (35.41%). It was inferred from the results of characterization that the improved photocatalytic activity of the GQDs/TiO2 NTs composites was attributed to the synergetic effect of up-conversion properties of the GQDs, enhanced visible light absorption and efficient separation of photogenerated electron-holes of the GQDs/TiO2 composite.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
TiO2 nanotubes
Composite
Photocatalysis

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of high quality yellow GQDs
Synthesis of GQDs with TiO2 NTs (GQDs/TiO2 NTs)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The synthesis procedures of OGO, GQDs, TiO2 NTs and GQDs/TiO2 NTs.
(a) Photograph of the GQDs and characters written on the filter paper using GQDs solution under UV irradiation at 365nm. (b) TEM image of GQDs; (c) HRTEM image of GQDs.

(a, b) PL spectra of GQDs aqueous solution at different excitation wavelengths; (c) Effect of pH value on the PL intensity of GQDs at various pHs (λex =320nm). (d) PL spectrum of GQDs with the detection wavelength of 430nm and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectrum excited at 320nm.

(a, b) SEM images of TiO2 NTs at different magnification; (c, d) TEM images of 2.5% GQDs/TiO2 NTs at different magnification; (e) The HRTEM image of the selected area for 2.5% GQDs/TiO2 NTs composite; (f) SAED pattern of 2.5% GQDs/TiO2 NTs.
(a) EDX energy spectrum. (b) SEM image. (c-e) EDX elemental maps of O, Ti and C in the GQDs/TiO2 NTs.
TGA curves of TiO2 NTs and GQDs/TiO2 NTs.
XRD patterns (a) and FTIR spectra (b) of GQDs, TiO2 NTs and GQDs/TiO2 NTs composites.
Nitrogen gas adsorption-desorption isotherms (measured at 77K) for TiO2 NTs and GQDs/TiO2 NTs composites.
(a)UV–vis spectra of GQDs, TiO2 NTs and GQDs/TiO2 NTs; (b) PL spectra of TiO2 NTs and GQDs/TiO2 NTs irradiated at 320nm.

(a) UV–vis spectra of original MO and MO after 20min degradation by TiO2 NTs and GQDs/TiO2 NTs. (b) UV–vis spectra of the MO aqueous solution after degradation for different time in the presence of 2.5% GQDs/TiO2 NTs; (c) the photocatalytic degradation of MO and (d) photodegradation rate of MO for TiO2 NTs and GQDs/TiO2 NTs composites. (e) kinetic plots for TiO2 NTs and GQDs/TiO2 NTs. (f) photo degradation stability of 2.5% GQDs/TiO2 NTs.
FTIR spectra (a) and XRD patterns (b) of the 2.5% GQDs/TiO2 NTs used before and after.
The degradation rates of MO by TiO2 and 2.5% GQDs/TiO2 under radiating light with 380–780nm and 420–780nm wavelength, respectively.
The illustration of enhanced light driven photocatalytic activity for GQDs/TiO2 NTs composite.
The calculated quantum yield of the GQDs.
The degradation rates of MO by TiO2 and 2.5% GQDs/TiO2 under radiating light with 380–780nm and 420–780nm wavelength, respectively.

head.ce:title: High quantum yield graphene quantum dots decorated TiO2 nanotubes for enhancing photocatalytic activity

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Department, Jinan University, Guangzhou 510632, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with high quantum yield (about 23.6% at an excitation wavelength of 320nm) and GQDs/TiO2 nanotubes (GQDs/TiO2 NTs) composites were achieved by a simple hydrothermal method at low temperature. Photoluminescence characterization showed that the GQDs exhibited the down-conversion PL features at excitation from 300 to 420nm and up-conversion photoluminescence in the range of 600–800nm. The photocatalytic activity of prepared GQDs/TiO2 NTs composites on the degradation of methyl orange (MO) was significantly enhanced compared with that of pure TiO2 nanotubes (TiO2 NTs). For the composites coupling with 1.5%, 2.5% and 3.5% GQDs, the degradation of MO after 20min irradiation under UV–vis light irradiation (λ=380–780nm) were 80.52%, 94.64% and 51.91%, respectively, which are much higher than that of pure TiO2 NTs (35.41%). It was inferred from the results of characterization that the improved photocatalytic activity of the GQDs/TiO2 NTs composites was attributed to the synergetic effect of up-conversion properties of the GQDs, enhanced visible light absorption and efficient separation of photogenerated electron-holes of the GQDs/TiO2 composite.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic reactions driven by sunlight represent a promising way to address the increasing environmental and energy concerns. Therefore, developing and optimizing highly efficient photocatalysts for photodegradation of organic pollutants and photocatalytic hydrogen evolution from water under visible light has attracted worldwide attention. To date, various photocatalytic semiconductors, including multi-metal oxides, graphitic carbon nitride, sulfides and oxynitrides, have been successfully developed [1–3]. Undoubted, titanium dioxide (TiO2) is one of the most widely studied photocatalysts due to its abundance, low cost, low toxicity, superior photostability, and high intrinsic catalytic activity under UV illumination, which offers a facile and inexpensive method for removing environmental pollutants relating to wastewater and polluted air [4–8]. Among all of the TiO2 nanostructured materials, vertically aligned TiO2 nanotubes or nanorods have gained considerable attentions as they can offer a large interior and outer surface area and provide a direct pathway for the transfer of photoexcited electrons, which causes the enhancement of light absorption and carrier separation [9]. In addition, the oriented nanotube structure can reduce the loss of light reflection under illumination, as the exterior photons that enter the internal nanotubes are less likely to escape because of multiple radiation scattering by the walls [5]. Nevertheless, it is a pity that the poor responses to visible light of TiO2 caused by its wide band gap, and the fast recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs had significantly hindered the application of pure TiO2 in photocatalysis [10]. Therefore, to increase the activity, many strategies, such as introducing heteroatoms [11,12], coupling with other semiconductors or dyes [13–15], controlling morphology [16] have been used. Especially, TiO2 sensitized with narrow-bandgap semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) (such as CdS [17,18] and CdSe [19]) have attracted much attention as the semiconductor QDs possess tunable bandgap by sizes, higher extinction coefficients than traditional dyes, and multiple exciton generation [19,20]. For the semiconductor QD-sensitized TiO2 NTs, the visible light can be fully used and the recombination of photocarriers can be effectively suppressed, therefore resulting in significant improvement of the photocatalytic activity of the TiO2 NTs. However, most of these semiconductor QD sensitizers are either highly unstable during light irradiation or poisonous, thus limiting their practical applications [21].

Recently, carbon quantum dots (CQDs, including graphene quantum dots (GQDs)) have received considerable attention because of their many unique properties. As an attractive material distinct from the metal-based quantum dots, the GQDs not only exhibit excellent solubility, chemical inertness, stable photoluminescence and fine biocompatibility, as well as low toxicity, but also possess other distinct merits, such as interesting size-dependent, pH-dependent and wavelength-dependent of down-conversion and up-conversion photoluminescence [22–27]. These particular characteristics endow GQDs with many potential applications, such as bioimaging [28–35], solar cells [36–41], biosensing [42–46], and photocatalysis [7,47–49]. As a powerful energy-transfer component, CQDs have been used to design photocatalyst with enhanced photocatalytic abilities by combining with semiconductor nanoparticles, such as SiO2 [25], TiO2 [9,49–66], Cu2O [67], BiVO4 [68], ZnO [69] and Fe2O3 [70] etc. Ming et al. [49] have reported a fabrication of carbon QDs/TiO2 nanoparticles composites through hydrothermal method and studied the visible light photocatalytic activity of the composites. Zhuo et al. [50] have found an ultrasonic route for fabrication of GQDs and prepared GQDs/TiO2 nanoparticles composites for degradation of MB. Li et al. [9] constructed unique coaxial heterogeneous-structured GQDs/TiO2 NTs by a coupling technique of silane linker molecule modification. The amine groups of the KH550 can covalently integrate with the carboxylic acid groups of the electrophoretic GQDs to produce firmly coalescent heterojunctions of GQDs/TiO2 NTs with amide groups. Rodenas et al. [65] showed that the hybrid TiO2/QDs photoanodes can produce hydrogen without the assistance of any external bias. Moreover, a detailed understanding of the electronic processes leading to H2 generation is presented based on physical modeling fed by impedance spectroscopy characterization. Even though there are several reports to produce TiO2-based visible light driven photocatalysts linked with the GQDs [9,51–53,56,60,62,63,65], the low quantum yield and complicated preparation process of GQDs still call for alternative methods. And it is still necessary to research the properties and mechanism on photocatalysis of the GQDs/TiO2 composites with high efficient photocatalytic activity.

Here, the yellow fluorescence GQDs with high quantum yield of 23.6% were achieved by one-step hydrothermal process using oxygen-rich graphene oxide (OGO), which can retain stably in water for three months without any changes. It is the first time to demonstrate that high concentration yellow GQDs solution and TiO2 NTs were obtained by simply one-step hydrothermal method. The GQDs/TiO2 NTs composite was also achieved via a hydrothermal dip method by immersing TiO2 NTs in GQDs solution. The properties and improved photocatalytic activity of the composites with different weight content of GQDs on the MO degradation were investigated. It is believable that our work will be helpful for the better design of visible light-driven photocatalysts of TiO2 composites.

Graphite powder (<5μm) was purchased from Nanjing XFNANO Materials Tech Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). TiO2 (P25) was purchased from Degussa. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 99%) and concentrated hydrochloric acid (HCl, 36.5%) were purchased from Beijing Chemical Reagent Company. Sodium nitrate (NaNO3, 99%) was purchased from Tianjin Fu Yu Fine Chemical Co., Ltd. Methyl orange (MO, 99%) was purchased from Aladdin. All reagents are used without further purification.

Based on the modified Hummers method [71,72], OGO was prepared by using graphene oxide (GO) as a raw material. 3g of graphite powder was added to an ice solution of concentrated H2SO4 (75mL) and NaNO3 (1.5g) in 500mL round-bottomed flask in an ice bath, then keeping ultrasonic dispersion for 15min. The mixture was kept at 4°C for 2h and 85°C for 3h under stirring. After cooling to the room temperature, the mixture was diluted with 100mL of DI water and left overnight. The diluted suspension with brown color was treated with 3% hydrogen peroxide to reduce the residual permanganate and manganese dioxide to soluble manganese sulfate. After the treatment with hydrogen peroxide, the suspension turned to be yellow. Then the suspension was separated by low-speed centrifugation to obtain a yellow-brown filter cake, which was dispersed in 100mL (0.1molL−1) HCl solution for 2h. The dispersion liquid was washed with DI water by high-speed centrifuge for several times, until the pH value of the solution tuned to be 7. At last, the OGO was dispersed in 200mL DI water and stored for further applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of high quality yellow GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The yellow GQDs were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal process from OGO. Firstly, 50mL OGO solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave (100mL), and the suspension was dispersed with high-speed shearing machine for 30min. Subsequently, 5mL NaOH (1molL−1) was added into the autoclave and kept ultrasonic dispersion for 15min. Then, the mixture was heated up to 200°C for 24h in an electronic oven. After being cooled to room temperature, the mixture was filtered using a 0.22μm microporous membrane and the yellow filter solution was obtained, which still contained small fluorescent substance. So, the yellow solution was further dialyzed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 500Da) for 24h and GQDs with strong fluorescent remaining in the bag was obtained with a yield of ca. 23.6%. The water in the GQDs solution was removed through freeze-drying. Finally, a pure honeycomb yellow solid of graphene quantum dots was obtained.

TiO2 NTs were synthesized by P25 as follows. 0.6g P25 powder was added to aqueous solution of 60mL NaOH (10molL−1) with stirring for 1h. Then, the mixture was slowly added into 100mL autoclave under stirring and the autoclave was put into an electronic oven under 120°C for 24h. After cooling to the room temperature, the aqueous solution was separated by a high speed centrifugation and the sediment was purified with HCl solution (0.1molL−1) until pH=1. Then, it was washed with DI water for several times until its pH value tuned to 7. After drying for 4h, the sample was put into a muffle furnace to be kept for 2h under 450°C. The obtained white powders of TiO2 NTs were stored for further application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GQDs with TiO2 NTs (GQDs/TiO2 NTs)
ce:section.ce:para: 100mg powders of TiO2 NTs and a certain volume of GQDs (0.2mg/mL) dispersions (7.5, 12.5, 17.5mL, corresponding to the GQDs/TiO2 NTs composite with coupled GQDs of 1.5wt%, 2.5wt% and 3.5wt%, respectively) were mixed with DI water in a beaker to obtain final volume of 30mL. After the mixture was stirring for 1h, it was loaded into a 100mL Teflon-lined autoclave for further solvothermal reaction at 110°C for 2h. Then, the solution was centrifuged at 11,000rpm for 10min and the sediment was washed twice with DI water. The final GQDs/TiO2 NTs composite was dried at 80°C for further photocatalytic experiments and characterization.

The structural properties of the materials were analyzed by X-ray powder diffraction (D8 Focus) on an X-Pert Pro diffraction meter with Cu Kα radiation (λ=1.5406Å) at a scanning speed of 2min−1. FT-IR measurements were performed using a Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Nicolet 6700, 400–4000cm−1) with the samples dispersed in KBr pellets at 1:99 ratio. UV–vis spectra were measured using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV–vis-NIR spectrophotometer 5000) to study its optical absorption property. Photoluminescence spectra (PL) of the samples were obtained using a fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi F-4500) at 293K. The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were obtained using a JEOL 2010-H microscope TEM operating at 200kV. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images, energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) pattern and EDX map were performed on a field emission-scanning electron microscopy (PHILIPS XL-30ESEM, Netherlands) at an accelerating voltage of 15kV. The samples for the analysis were prepared by dropping GQDs/TiO2 NTs dispersion in ethanol onto 400-mesh carbon-coated copper grids and leaving the solvent to dry.

The degradation of MO dyes under UV–vis light irradiation (λ=380–780nm) was tested by a CEL-HX photochemical reactor system irradiated by a 300W Xe lamp. Aqueous solution of the MO dyes (100mL of 14mgL−1 solution) and the photocatalyst (GQDs/TiO2 NTs) of 0.1g was placed into a quartz glass reactor of 200mL. After stirring for 1h in the dark, the photocatalytic reaction was started by turning on the lamp. After a specified time, the 4mL photo-reacted solution was analyzed by recording variations of the maximum absorption band in the UV–vis spectra of MO.

The quantum yield (Q) of GQDs was determined by using quinine sulfate in 0.1M H2SO4 as the standard sample and calculated according to the following Eq. (1), where Q is the quantum yield, n is the refractive index, A is the absorbance, and I is the measured integrated emission intensity. The subscript R refers to the reference fluorophore of known quantum yield. (n=nR =1.3). Quinine sulfate in 0.1M H2SO4 solution (Q=at 320 nm) was chosen as a standard.
Q =
Q
R
I
I R
A R
A
n
2
n R
2
The explanation of the quantum yield measurement:

The fluorescence quantum yield of GQDs was obtained by comparing with quinine sulfate as a standard under the same excitation conditions [102,103]. First, the absorbance of a sample at a specific wavelength of excitation was measured. Then the emission spectrum of the sample under certain excitation wavelength was tested. Integrated fluorescence intensity (I) of the sample can be calculated by integrating the area below the peak emission. The fluorescence quantum yield of quinine sulfate was 55.7% as illustrated in Table 1 and the corresponding data of quinine sulfate as standard values can be obtained from the previous reference [104] as given in the Table S1.
The fluorescence quantum yield of GQDs sample was calculated as the followings:

According to the referenced absorbance value of quinine sulfate, the absorbance of GQDs was adjusted to ensure the difference between the absorbance values of GQDs and quinine sulfate to be smaller than 0.05 under the corresponding exciting wavelength. The absorbance of GQDs solution (A) at 320nm exciting wavelength is 0.032 and the UV–Vis spectrum of GQDs was shown in Fig. S1. Then, the fluorescence emission peak of this solution was measured as shown in red line in Fig. 2d of the manuscript. The corresponding peak area between 380nm and 580nm wavelength was integrated by using the Origin 8.6, which is equal to 79765.
At last, the quantum yield of GQDs was calculated to be 23.6% according to the Eq. (1).

The synthesis procedures of OGO, GQDs, TiO2 NTs and GQDs/TiO2 NTs are illustrated in Scheme 1 . The GQDs with high quantum yield were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal process using OGO, which was obtained from GO based on modified Hummer method [71,72]. After carefully getting rid of the deposition and some other impurities, the solid GQDs can be dispersed in water showing light coffee (as in Scheme 1) and kept stable for a long time. The GQDs solution emitted yellow fluorescence under UV irradiation at 365nm as shown in Fig. 1 a. We also can see the fluorescence word “GQDs” clearly under the ultraviolet light with 365nm wavelength written on the filter paper using GQDs solution as fluorescent ink, indicating the achievement of the high concentration GQDs. Interestingly, most of the resulting carbon dots and GQDs exhibit blue or green emission, despite distinct techniques for fabrication [73]. Different from that of blue-luminescent GQDs synthesized hydrothermally in previous reports [74], as well as the yellow GQDs synthesized by electrochemically exfoliating graphite [34], the synthesized GQDs here excited by 365nm lamp emitted yellow luminescence, which should be attributed to the effect of size and surface functional groups. Fig. 1b shows the TEM images of prepared GQDs. It can be seen that the GQDs are dispersed uniformly without clear aggregations. The high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image in Fig. 1c shows that the lattice spacing is about 0.21nm, which is resulted from the in-plane lattice spacing of graphene (100 facet) [38]. It was revealed that the sizes of GQDs are from 3 to 5nm, much smaller than those of GQDs (around 9.6nm) synthesized by hydrothermal methods [74].

To further explore the optical properties of synthesized GQDs, a detailed PL study was carried out by using different excitation wavelengths. As shown in Fig. 2 a, with the increase of excited wavelengths from 300 to 420nm, the PL peak intensity of the GQDs increased to the maximum at excited wavelength of 320nm and then decreased. In accord with the previous report [74], the PL spectrum exhibits the strongest emission peak centered at 430nm without evident shift under the excitation wavelength from 300 to 340nm. When the excitation wavelength was changed from 340 to 420nm, the PL peaks shifted to longer wavelengths with wide bands and the strongest peaks centered at about 500nm and their intensity decreased rapidly. The observed photoluminescence red-shift of GQDs was accompanied by a significant reduction in photoluminescence intensity that is a signature of the gradual evolution of the confinement in quantum dots [75]. In Fig. 2b, the emission peaks intensity of the GQDs increased with excitation from 600 to 650nm without shift and then decreased with red-shift by varying excitation from 650 to 800nm. The spectra show the strongest emission peaks centered at about 420nm below the excitation wavelength of 650nm and a red-shift above 650nm. These results indicated the PL characteristic of excitation-dependent, which displayed down-converted PL property under the excitation wavelength from 300 to 420nm and up-converted PL property under the excitation wavelength from 600 to 800nm. This is consistent with the results of previous reports [31,38,74,76,77]. The excitation-dependent emission properties of GQDs may be related to their highly defective structures with a great number of sp3 and vacancy defect states in the basal plane [78,79]. Moreover, the GQDs exhibited apparent pH-independent feature as shown in Fig. 2c. The GQDs emit strong PL under alkaline conditions, whereas the PL intensity became gradually attenuated under acidic conditions. If the pH is switched repeatedly between 13 and 1, the PL intensity varies reversibly. This is consistent with the previous reports on GQDs [28,31,74,80]. However, different from the result in previous report [81], the emission peak of our synthesized GQDs shows a slightly blue-shift with the increase of pH value, revealing their pH-dependent character. In acidic conditions, the PL intensity attenuated, which is closely related to change the GQDs surface structural characteristics. The free zigzag sites of the GQDs are protonated, forming a reversible complex between the zigzag sites and H+. Thus the emissive triple carbene state is broken and becomes inactive in PL [25]. Varying the pH of the solution, corresponding to the deprotonation of the carboxyl groups on the surface of the GQDs, might cause electrostatic doping/charging to the GQDs and shift the Fermi level [82]. The free zigzag sites are restored, thereby leading to the restoration of PL. The PLE spectrum of the GQDs (λem =320nm) as well as the PL spectrum is displayed in Fig. 2d. The PL excitation (PLE) spectrum recorded with the strongest luminescence shows a sharp peak at 320nm, which corresponds to the 320nm absorption band. The PL quantum yield (QY) of the GQDs excited at 320nm was estimated to be about 23.6%, which is much higher than those of GQDs reported previously [25,28,74,83]. The as-prepared GQDs solution is transparent, and exhibits strong PL without any perceptible change of fluorescence intensity even after 90 days in air at room temperature, indicating good fluorescent stability.

The optical properties suggest that GQDs may be used as a powerful energy-transfer component in photocatalyst design for applications in environmental and energy issues. Therefore, we expected to combine GQDs with TiO2 to achieve the efficient usage of the most spectrum of sunlight. At present, TiO2 NTs reported in previous papers were synthesized by electrochemical methods [25,38,84,85]. In this work, a different way was used to prepare TiO2 NTs by hydrothermal reaction to form tubes, which was then calcined in a muffle furnace. The SEM images in Fig. 3 a and b revealed the dense and non-uniform growth of tubes, with diameter of 30–50nm and length of ∼3μm. To demonstrate the successful loading of the GQDs on the TiO2 NTs, we carried out TEM and HRTEM images of TiO2 NTs loaded with GQDs as shown in Fig. 3c–e. The HRTEM image showed that the GQDs (lattice spacing 0.21nm) were in close contact with the TiO2 NTs with the (004) crystal plane exposed (lattice spacing 0.24nm) which constitutes the tube wall of the TiO2 NTs [86]. These results confirmed that the GQDs were successfully loaded onto the surface of TiO2 NTs surfaces. The well-separated diffraction spots in the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern as Fig. 3f shows the single-crystalline nature of GQDs and polycrystal character of TiO2 NTs.

To further confirm the successful loading of GQDs on TiO2 NTs, the SEM image and EDX element maps of GQDs/TiO2 NTs composites were obtained and shown in Fig. 4 . EDX element measurement confirmed the existence of C, O, and Ti elements in the GQDs/TiO2 NTs composites, indicating that GQDs had attached to the surface of TiO2 NTs.

Because the EDX analysis only exhibits the GQDs existence on the surface of TiO2 NTs and is not suitable used as quantitative analysis method, we further did the TGA analysis to confirm the weight ratios of GQDs in composites. Seen from Fig. 5 , the weight loss below 100°C is perhaps due to the structural water adsorption and it is ascribed to the decomposition of GQDs above 100°C [87]. For the composites coupled with 7.5mL, 12.5mL and 17.5mL addition volume of GQDs solution, the weight losses are 1.14%, 2.22% and 3.07%, respectively, which further suggested that most of the GQDs have been doped on the TiO2 NTs.

The chemical interactions between TiO2 NTs and GQDs are important for the stability and properties of the composites. To demonstrate the structure of GQDs/TiO2 NTs, the XRD and FTIR were analyzed. As seen from Fig. 6 a, the XRD pattern of GQDs showed a wide weak diffraction peak centered at around 25 corresponding to the [002] facet. This is in agreement with the interlay distance of graphene sheets, demonstrating the graphitic nature of the crystalline dots [88]. The XRD pattern of the TiO2 NTs corresponds to a typical diffraction pattern of anatase phase TiO2. The peak at 25.4 ascribed to the (101) plane diffraction of anatase TiO2. The diffraction peak at 38.1, 48.3, 54.2, 55.4, 62.8, 69.1, 70.5 and 75.3 can be observed obviously, corresponding to the (004), (200), (105), (211), (204), (116), (220) and (215) crystal faces of anatase TiO2 (JCPDS card No. 21–1272). In addition, GQDs/TiO2 NTs composites showed similar XRD pattern with pure TiO2 NTs, suggesting that the presence of GQDs in the GQDs/TiO2 NTs composites did not affect the crystalline structure of TiO2 NTs, which might be due to the low amount and relatively low diffraction intensity of GQDs [52]. Though the peak of GQDs was not observed in the GQDs/TiO2 NTs, the diffraction peak intensity of composites decreased obviously with the quantity of GQDs increased. The possible reason is that the production of crystal orientation (002) in GQDs leads to the decrease of TiO2 NTs peaks.

The FT-IR spectra of GQDs and GQDs/TiO2 NTs composites in Fig. 6b showed the typical band at 3441cm−1 assigned to the OH groups and a strong peak at 1571cm−1 ascribed to the vibration of CC bonds. The peak at 1418cm−1 is due to COH or CH stretching vibration and the peak at 1106cm−1 can be assigned to CO stretching vibration [89]. These peaks suggest that GQDs contain –OH and –COOH groups, which is contributed to the oxidation cutting of graphene oxide that led to the break-up of sp2 domains into quantum dots [90]. The carboxyl and hydroxyl groups existed in GQDs are in favour of the stability of the GQDs in aqueous solution. The FTIR of pure TiO2 NTs showed abroad peak at 400–800cm−1 accords to the bonds of TiO and TiOTi [91]. Compared to the TiO2 NTs, this vibration band for GQDs/TiO2 NTs shows a slight red shift which is caused by the combination of GQDs and the vibration of Ti OC vibration [92]. This confirmed that the GQDs are coordinated with TiO2 NTs.

The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of TiO2 NTs and GQDs/TiO2 NTs composites were shown in Fig. 7 . The increase in the nitrogen sorption at a high relative pressure above 0.9 may arise in part from interparticulate porosity associated with the meso- and macrostructures of the samples and interparticular void [93]. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface area of TiO2 NTs, 1.5% GQDs/TiO2, 2.5% GQDs/TiO2 and 3.5% GQDs/TiO2 composites are 122.70, 123.80, 127.65 and 130.08m2 g−1, respectively. The surface area increased slightly with the increase of weight ratio of coupling quantum dots.

The optical properties of GQDs, TiO2 NTs and GQDs/TiO2 NTs composites are characterized by UV–vis absorption and PL spectroscopy as shown in Fig. 8 . It can be seen from Fig. 8a that there is no absorption band in the visible light of as-prepared GQDs. Only a broad and weak absorption shoulder peak below 270nm may be attributed to π–π* electron transition of graphitic sp2 domains as reported [34]. The UV–vis spectrum of TiO2 NTs exhibited no absorption in the region of above 400nm wavelength, which accords with the intrinsic absorption property of the pure TiO2 [94]. Whereas all GQDs/TiO2 NTs composites displayed obviously absorption increase in visible region and a little red-shift, which indicated that GQDs can broaden the photo-response range of the TiO2 NTs to visible and near infrared light. The absorption intensity showed the strongest when the weight ratio of the GQDs in the composites was 2.5%. The PL emission spectra of GQDs/TiO2 NTs composites were investigated to confirm the electron transfer. As shown in Fig. 8b, the PL spectrum of TiO2 NTs presents two main peaks at 380 and 445nm, which can be assigned to the direct bandgap transition and the oxygen vacancy with one trapped electron, respectively [95]. After anchoring the GQDs, a significant attenuation of PL is observed for all composites. The composite of 2.5% GQDs/TiO2 shows the weakest PL intensity. However, the PL peak at 430nm from GQDs is not obvious for all the GQDs/TiO2 NTs composites irradiated at 320nm, which is probably covered by TiO2. The PL test confirms that the GQDs can act as a photosensitizer to accept the excited electron transferred from TiO2 to improve the separation of photogenerated electron-holes, which is in good accordance with the earlier reports [96].

We used the degradation reaction of methyl orange (MO) to evaluate the photocatalytic activity of the GQDs/TiO2 NTs composites under UV–vis light irradiation λ=380–780nm). It took 1h for MO to reach adsorption equilibrium in dark. During this adsorption equilibrium, there is no obvious color change of MO. After this, the photocatalytic degradation timing was started by turning on the lamp. Fig. 9 a shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of MO before and after degradation for 20min by the composites and pure TiO2. We can see that the absorption peak intensity of MO is at 475nm. After degradation of MO for 20min, the intensity of absorption peaks of all composites displayed more attenuated than that of pure TiO2. Obviously, the 2.5% GQDs/TiO2 NTs showed the most efficient light degradation. The 94.64% of MO are degraded after 20min of irradiation, which is higher than that of pure TiO2 NTs and other composites. Fig. 9b displayed the UV–vis spectroscopic changes of the MO aqueous solution in the presence of 2.5% GQDs/TiO2 NTs. It is clear that the peak intensity of MO decrease to quenching after degradation for 20min. The standardization concentration changes (C/C0) of MO during the degradation process were proportional to the standardization maximum absorbance value (A/A0), hence, the relative concentration of MO at a given time interval was removed and detected. The TiO2 NTs and GQDs/TiO2 NTs composites induced photocatalytic degradation of MO under UV–vis light illumination were shown in Fig. 9c. The results displayed that about 98% of MO can be degraded by TiO2 NTs after 90min irradiation. In Fig. 9d, it can be seen that the GQDs/TiO2 NTs composites have higher degradation rate than pure TiO2 nanotubes. For the composites coupling with 1.5%, 2.5% and 3.5% GQDs, the degradation of MO after 20min irradiation were 80.52%, 94.64% and 51.91%, respectively, which are much higher than that of pure TiO2 NTs (35.41%). For the composites of coupling with 1.5%, 2.5%, 3.5% GQDs, respectively, 98% of MO has been degraded only within 40min, 20min, 60min, which are faster than that of pure TiO2 NTs. According to the photocatalytic degradation kinetic curves shown in Fig. 9e, the photocatalytic reactions followed pseudo first-order kinetics before approaching to thoroughly degradation. Generally equation dC/dt=kC was simply described as the photocatalytic reaction, where C is the concentration of MO and k represents the pseudo first-order rate constant. The constants k (min−1) values for TiO2 NTs, 1.5% GQDs/TiO2, 2.5% GQDs/TiO2 and 3.5% GQDs/TiO2 in the degradation of MO within 60min are estimated to be 0.0307, 0.0844, 0.0861, and 0.0671min−1, respectively. From the results of photocatalytic kinetic constants, the photocatalytic activity of the composites increased with the increase of doping amount of GQDs to 2.5% and decrease gradually afterwards. It indicated that the optimum coupled quantity of GQDs is 2.5% in our experiment. This variation trend is well consistent with the earlier results, even though the morphology of TiO2 is different [97,98].

It is evident that the doping of GQDs can improve the photocatalytic activity of pure TiO2 NTs. A small amount of GQDs on the surface of TiO2 can improve the light adsorption and enhance the separation efficiency of electrons and holes, which leads to an improved photocatalytic activity. Whereas excessive anchored GQDs also occupy the main photocatalytic active sites in the surface of TiO2, leading to a high electron-hole recombination and a poor photocatalytic activity [98].

The photocatalytic degradation of MO was performed for four cycles to test the stability of 2.5% GQDs/TiO2 NTs composite as shown in Fig. 9f. It has no obvious decrease during the four recycling, suggesting good photocatalytic stability of the composites. The structure of the used 2.5% GQDs/TiO2 NTs composite was also characterized by FTIR and XRD, which were illustrated in Fig. 10 . It can be seen that there is no obvious difference between the 2.5% GQDs/TiO2 NTs used before and after, further confirming the stability of the photocatalysts. At the same time, the blank test showed that there isn't any degradation of MO under UV–vis light radiating for 1.5h, when the photocatalyst was not present.

The photocatalytic degradation activity of the TiO2 and 2.5% GQDs/TiO2 under visible light radiation (420–780nm) was compared with that under UV–vis light radiation (380–780nm). The results were given in Table 2 and Fig. 11 , which showed that there is only a little difference. This indicated that most activity of the photocatalyst is resulted from the radiation under visible light.

Based on above results, the possible mechanism for the enhanced photocatalytic activity of the GQDs/TiO2 NTs composites is illustrated in Fig. 12 , which can be described by the following three features: firstly, the coupling of GQDs with TiO2 NTs enhanced the absorption of TiO2 in the range of visible light, due to the electronic coupling between π states of the graphite-essential GQDs and conduction band states of TiO2 [84]. Secondly, GQDs can act as an electron reservoir to trap photogenerated electrons from TiO2 NTs and promote the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs [54], which is confirmed by PL measurement. In addition, the up-conversion properties of the GQDs will transfer the long wavelength irradiation light into the shorter wavelength emission light to be used efficiently by GQDs/TiO2 NTs composites. O2 absorbed on the surface of the GQDs can accept e− and form O2 − which then oxidizes MO directly on the surface [49]. At the same time, the hole in the surface of TiO2 further oxidized H2O/OH to produce OH radicals, which would contribute to decompose the MO dye in water into H2O and CO2 [97]. Most previous publications have focused on the investigations on the photodegradation mechanism of MO [66,99–101]. In general, there are two approaches for the degradation of MO: one is the Azo-type degradation in neutral and alkaline conditions; the other is the Quinoid degradation in acidic conditions. Based on these papers, we concluded the degradation mechanism of MO in our experiment followed the Azo-type degradation mechanism.

In summary, we synthesized the high concentration and yellow fluorescence GQDs by a simple low-cost method. The quantum yield of GQDs was up to 23.6% at an excitation wavelength of 320nm, which was a relatively high yield for the production of GQDs. The TEM and HRTEM illustrate their uniformity and good dispersion, with diameters from 3 to 5nm. Their optical properties were evaluated in detail. The GQDs/TiO2 NTs composite were synthesized and characterized by FT-IR, SEM, HRTEM, EDX, TGA and XRD analyses. These results indicated that GQDs deposited on TiO2 NTs can obvious broaden the visible light absorption of TiO2 NTs and enhance the activity on photocatalytic degradation of MO under UV–vis light irradiation λ=380–780nm). What's more, the 2.5wt% GQDs/TiO2 NTs showed the best degradation ability, which is about 2.7 times as high as that of the pure TiO2 NTs. Furthermore, the possible mechanism for the enhanced photocatalytic activity of the GQDs/TiO2 NTs composites was proposed, which attributed to three aspects from enhanced visible light absorption, separation of photogenerated electron-holes and up-conversion properties of the GQDs. Future work should be focused on further improvement of photocatalytic activity on degradation of waste organic matter and hydrogen evolution from water.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2016.03.077.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:
